coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent S-doped carbon dots for the selective detection of ammonia
coredata.dc:creator: Travlou, Nikolina A.
Secor, Jeff
Bandosz, Teresa J.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (S-CDs) were obtained from two different S-containing polymer precursors using a hydrothermal treatment. They were used as highly sensitive and selective ammonia sensors based on a change in the fluorescence intensity. The gas detection test was carried out through the direct insertion of gaseous ammonia into the CD solutions. The results showed a time-dependent (concentration dependent) fluorescence quenching in the presence of ammonia. The detection time was as low as 30 s. The fluorescence quenching was linked to the involvement of sulfur and oxygen containing surface functional groups in a chemical reaction with NH3. In particular, sulfonic and carboxylic acids participated in acid-base reactions with ammonia to form ammonium salts. In the reaction of the target gas with carboxylic acids/acid anhydrites or phenols, cyclic ethers and ketone groups (diketones), amides and amines were formed, respectively. Strongly acidic sulfones and sulfonic acids played the most crucial role. The sample with the highest contribution of those groups showed the highest affinity for the reaction with ammonia, and thus the highest sensitivity among two samples tested.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ammonia sensing on S1-CDs and S2-CDs
Characterization of S1-CDs and S2-CDs after ammonia exposure
Characterization and sensing capability of N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Division of Chemical, Bioengineering, Environmental, and Transport Systems

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the ARO grant W911NF-13-1-0225, and NSF collaborative CBET Grant no. 1133112. The authors would like to thank Prof. Stephen O'Brien, Julien Lombardi and Frederick Pearsall for accommodating our needs to use Thermolyne, 6000 furnace.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the prepared S1-CDs and S2-CDs. HRTEM images are included as inserts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A, B): 3-D excitation emission spectra for S1-CDs and S2-CDs dots. The excitation is along the vertical direction and the emission is along the horizontal one. (C, D): Individual emission plots (photoluminescent spectra) for various excitation wavelengths. (E): The emission spectra of S1-CDs and S2-CDs, and the UV–visible absorption spectra of S1-CDs. The insert is a photograph of CD solutions under ambient and UV light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A): FT-IR spectra for S1-CDs and S2-CD aqueous solutions. The water spectra is subtracted. (B) FT-IR spectra of initial S1-CDs and S2-CDs those after drying in air. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
C 1s, O 1s, and S 2p core level spectra of S1-CDs and S2-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
EDX elemental mapping of the S1-CDs and S2-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Time-dependent fluorescence change of S1-CDs upon exposure to 300 ppm of ammonia, (B) Dependence of the fluorescence intensity on various response times (dashed line: excludes the maximum intensity after the 15 and 30 min exposure (seen as open symbols in Fig. 6B), solid line: includes all values of maximum intensity), (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the S1-CDs with respect to various ammonia concentrations, with a response time of 3 min, (D) Dependence of the fluorescence intensity on various ammonia concentrations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Time-dependent fluorescence change of S2-CDs upon exposure to 300 ppm of ammonia, (B) Dependence of the fluorescence intensity on various response times, (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the S1-CDs with respect to various ammonia concentrations, with a response time of 3 min, (D) Dependence of the fluorescence intensity on various ammonia concentrations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra for initial and exposed to ammonia S1-CDs and S2-CDs.

Energy states of the CDs before and after ammonia exposure. Prior to ammonia exposure, the radiative defect states of the CDs give rise to the broad visible fluorescence. After ammonia exposure, the formed defects become non-radiative trap states that partially quench the energy. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FT-IR spectra or N-CDs (A). A TEM image of the N-CDs as well as photographs of the N-CD solution before and after irradiation with UV light (B). Fluorescence emission spectra of S1-CDs at various concentrations of ammonia (C) and hydrogen sulfide (D) with a response time of 3 min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Content of elements on the surface and the O/C ratio (in at. % from XPS analysis).
The results of deconvolution of C 1s, O 1s and S 2p core energy levels.

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent S-doped carbon dots for the selective detection of ammonia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The City College of New York, New York, NY, 10031, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The City College of New York

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ph.D. Program in Chemistry, The Graduate Center of the City University of New York, New York, NY, 10016, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Graduate Center of the City University of New York

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, The City College of New York, New York, NY, 10031, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The City College of New York

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, The City College of New York, New York, NY, 10031, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The City College of New York

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (S-CDs) were obtained from two different S-containing polymer precursors using a hydrothermal treatment. They were used as highly sensitive and selective ammonia sensors based on a change in the fluorescence intensity. The gas detection test was carried out through the direct insertion of gaseous ammonia into the CD solutions. The results showed a time-dependent (concentration dependent) fluorescence quenching in the presence of ammonia. The detection time was as low as 30 s. The fluorescence quenching was linked to the involvement of sulfur and oxygen containing surface functional groups in a chemical reaction with NH3. In particular, sulfonic and carboxylic acids participated in acid-base reactions with ammonia to form ammonium salts. In the reaction of the target gas with carboxylic acids/acid anhydrites or phenols, cyclic ethers and ketone groups (diketones), amides and amines were formed, respectively. Strongly acidic sulfones and sulfonic acids played the most crucial role. The sample with the highest contribution of those groups showed the highest affinity for the reaction with ammonia, and thus the highest sensitivity among two samples tested.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) [1–3] are a new form of fluorescent carbon nanostructures that has recently gained increased research interest. Their diameters are smaller than 10 nm and they are considered as superior to conventional heavy-metal semiconductor quantum dots owing to the low toxicity, high biocompatibility, water solubility, optical stability, easiness of functionalization/modification, and low costs [2,4]. Because of these unique properties, they have been applied in bioimaging [5,6], drug delivery [7], photocatalysis [8], electrocatalysis [9], optoelectronics [10], photovoltaics [11], energy conversion/storage [12], and sensing [5].

Various synthetic approaches including physical, chemical, and solvothermal treatments have been used for the preparation of fluorescent CDs with photoluminescent colors that range from the visible to the near-infrared region [13–16]. Various strategies lead to CDs with a broad range of quantum yield [17–20]. To increase it, a low-cost heteroatom-doping, which is an effective way to adjust the composition and structure of CDs, has been widely used [16,21–24]. Several preparation strategies have been reported in the literature on the doping of CDs with various heteroatoms such as N, S, B or P and on their influence on the optical properties [25–27]. The most common methods include solvothermal/hydrothermal treatment of heteroatom containing organic sources such as thiourea [21], carbon tetrachloride [28], 1,2-ethylendiamine [29], l-cysteine [30], formaldehyde [31], glucose [19], and folic [32], glutamic [33] or citric acid [22,30].

So far, many papers published in the literature focused on the detection of heavy metals [18,34–37], organic molecules [16,25] and biomolecules [38,39] using CDs. In their photoexcited states, CDs can act as either good electron donors or acceptors, and thus cause chemiluminescence and electrochemical luminescence [40]. Because of these unique photoelectronic properties, they can be considered as potential candidates for toxic gas sensors, especially in the case where electron donating or electron accepting gases, such as NH3/H2S or NO2 respectively, are the detection targets. Nevertheless, studies on the application of CDs as gas sensors have been rarely reported. Ganiga and Cyriac fabricated a Förster (fluorescence) resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based ammonia sensor [41]. A mixture of the CD solution and sodium rhodizonate was used as the sensing system. The detection mechanism was based on a decreased emission because of the energy transfer from the CDs to the sodium rhodizonate upon the introduction of ammonia. In another study, Jiang and coworkers reported the fabrication of a solid-state CD-based fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of ammonia [42]. CDs were derived from a sugar cane bagasse. The fluorescent quenching of the CDs was the result of the chemical interactions of their surface carboxylic groups with the target gas. No details, however, on the effects of surface chemical heterogeneity and on the amount of ammonia (in mg or mmol) that causes the saturation/exhaustion of the carbon dots and therefore diminishes their detection capability were provided.

Even though numerous works focus on the preparation and application of N-doped or N-and S-co-doped CDs, which were found to have enhanced fluorescence and electronic properties [11,21,22,43–46], fewer results have been reported on solely sulfur-containing CDs and their applications [27,45,47]. Li and coworkers synthesized sulfated carbon quantum dots, S-CQDs, which they tested as visible-light-induced acid photocatalysts [48]. They found that sulfated-CQDs exhibit a photo-induced variation in their acidity. This was related to the photoexcitation of S-CQD, which caused electron transfer from SO3 − to carbon. The suggested mechanism of photoexcitation and charge transfer in S-CQDs upon light irradiation is very similar to the one that our research group reported earlier for S- and N-doped nanoporous carbons [49,50]. We have indicated that the fluorescence capability of the S-doped carbons was related to the specific configuration of the heteroatoms. Sulfones, sulfoxides, and sulfonic acids, acting as chromophores played the most important role. Upon their irradiation with visible light an electron transfer from the chromophores to the graphitic domains of the carbon matrix resulted in photoluminescence. Considering that CQDs do not differ significantly in the surface chemistry from carbon, their luminescence properties might be affected by the same chemical features as those of porous carbon particles.

Herein we report the highly sensitive and selective ammonia sensing capability of two different S-containing CD samples with similar particle sizes. The ammonia concentration limits were selected following the OSHA standards (50 ppm is the permissible exposure limit and 500 ppm is the maximum short exposure tolerance) [51]. For their preparation a very simple hydrothermal treatment of two different low cost S-containing polymers was applied. Unlike in the previously reported studies where the gas detection tests were carried out either using the CD solution adsorbed over a cotton substrate [41], or coating a PVDF membrane [42], here, gaseous ammonia is directly introduced/purged into the solution of the CDs tested. The objective of this study is to examine the features of S-CDs that can be used in gas detection alarm applications, rather than introduce new ammonia sensors. The role of the specific surface chemistry of the dots (specific surface functional groups) is extensively analyzed and linked to their ammonia detection capability and fluorescence quenching.

The predetermined amount of poly(sodium4-styrene sulfonate) or poly(4-styrene sulfonic acid co-maleic acid) were dissolved in 50 mL of H2O, and the solutions were added to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was kept in a furnace at 200 °C for 6 h. The obtained mixture were first filtered using a cylindrical filtration membrane filter (0.22 mm), and then washed with dichloromethane to remove the unreacted organic moieties. Finally, for the separation of the less fluorescent deposit, they were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The concentration of the CD solutions was 2.9% w/v. For the preparation of the N-CDs a predetermined amount of urea was dissolved in water, and the solution was added to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in a furnace for 6 h at 200 °C. It was then filtered using a cylindrical filtration membrane filter (0.22 mm).
Image 2

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using a Nicolet Magna-IR 830 spectrometer, equipped with ZnSe mull omni-cell window in a transmission mode. The background spectra was collected both with and without using water as a blank solution. The spectra were collected 32 times and corrected for the background noise.

Fluorescence measurements were made in an aqueous solution in a 1 cm square quartz cuvette using a spectrophotomer (Fluoromax, Horiba Inc). The emission intensity was corrected for excitation power so that intensity comparisons could be made. The excitation-emission scans were made with an excitation increment of 5 nm.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed using a JEOL 2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The analyses were conducted both on liquid and dry carbon dots. In the latter case the dry dots were previously suspended in ethanol. EDX analysis was performed on the same instrument.

The quantum yield of the dots (ΦCD) was measured using quinine sulfate as a standard (ΦQ = 0.54) in 0.1 M H2SO4, according to the equation [52]:
Φ
C D
=
Φ Q
(
1 −
10
−
A Q
1 −
10
−
A
C D
)
(
n
C D
n Q
)
2
D
C D
D Q

In this equation Ф stands for the quantum yield, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and D the integrated spectral intensity. Quantum yield measurements were carried out using 350 nm excitation and identical instrument parameters for all samples. Blank tests of each solution were measured for baseline corrections.

The chemical composition of our samples was determined using a Physical Electronics PHI 5000 VersaProbe II spectrometer, with a Al Ka X-ray radiation source (50 W, 15 kV, 1486.6 eV). The XPS take-off angle was set at 45°, using a concentric hemispherical analyzer operating in constant-pass-energy mode at 29.35 eV, with a 200 μm diameter analysis area. Under these conditions, the Au 4f 7/2 line was recorded with 1.16 eV FWHM at a binding energy of 84.0 eV. The spectrometer energy scale was calibrated using Cu 2p 3/2, Ag 3d 5/2, and Au 4f 7/2 photoelectron lines at 932.7, 368.3, and 84.0 eV, respectively. The pressure in the analysis chamber was 7.4 × 10−8 Pa. For the acquisition and data analysis, the SmartSoft-VP2.6.3.4 software package was used. A Shirley-type background was subtracted from the signals. To determine the binding energy of the different element core levels accurately, the recorded spectra were always fitted using Gauss–Lorentz curves.

The ammonia sensing capability of the CD solutions was tested in two ways: in terms of the dependence of the fluorescence intensity on various exposure times, and in terms of the dependence of the fluorescence intensity on various concentrations of the target gas. In the former case, 3.2 cm3 portions of each CD solution were exposed to 300 ppm of ammonia bubbled through solutions for 30 s, 1 min, 2.5 min, 5 min, 7 min, 15 min, and 30 min. In the latter case, ammonia of various concentrations in air was bubbled through the 3.2 cm3 volumes of the CD solutions with 3 min exposure time. A schematic illustration of the experimental set-up is provided in Fig. S1 of the Supporting Information (SI).

Fig. 1 shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the as-prepared S1-CDs (A) and S2-CDs (B). High-resolution TEM images are included as inserts. These CDs were obtained from poly (sodium4-styrene sulfonate) and poly (4-styrene sulfonic acid co-maleic acid), respectively. As seen, the materials consist of spherical, uniform and well dispersed dots, with an average diameter smaller than 5 nm and typical to graphitic carbon lattice parameters.

Excitation-emission measurements were made in order to characterize the energy levels of CDs. The 3-dimensional plots of the excitation-emission scan are presented in Fig. 2 A and B. The vertical axis shows the excitation wavelengths while the horizontal axis shows the emission wavelengths. The emission intensities are plotted with a color gradient. The color scale bar for the intensity counts is given on the side of each image. A line crossing below the excitation-emission island represents the exciting light. S1-CDs have a maximum emission wavelength near 410 nm when excited with 350 nm light (Fig. 2A). The peak of the emission shifts with the excitation wavelength, and the dots are able to produce emission from excitation wavelengths up to 600 nm. S2-CDs dots have similar emission characteristics, however the maximum resonance is slightly redshifted with the emission maximum at 430 nm with an excitation of 370 nm (Fig. 2B). S2-CDs also produce emission when excited with wavelengths up to 650 nm. Fig. 2C and D, illustrate the photoluminescent (PL) spectra of S1- and S2-CDs respectively, for selected individual excitations, showing that for both samples the emission peak wavelength shifts with the excitation energy and that the emission is generated for wavelengths much beyond the maximum resonance. Even though the behavior of both samples is similar, a difference is noted and the reasons for that are discussed further in this paper.

The emission spectra of the CD solutions at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm are compared in Fig. 2E. Considering that both CD solutions have the same concentration (2.9% w/v), the differences in their intensities are irrelevant to it (concentration). Fig. 2E also illustrates the absorption spectra of S1and S2-CDs. The insert photograph shows the CD solutions under ambient and UV light. The same Figure also includes the measured pH values of the CD solutions, which were found to be 3.0 and 6.7, for S1-CDs and S2-CDs, respectively. The pH values directly indicate the differences in the CDs' surface chemistry, and the high intensity of S1-CDs might suggest its better suitability to sensing.

S1 and S2-CDs show moderate quantum yields (9% and 6%, respectively) using 350 nm excitation. These values are comparable to those reported in the literature for S or S,N-co-doped CDs. More precisely, H. Ding and coworkers reported a quantum yield of about 5.5% for S-doped CDs [45], S. Chandra and coworkers reported a value of about 11.8%, also for S-doped CDs [47], while D. Sun and coworkers found quantum yield values varying from 9 to 29% for S,N-CDs [53]. In the case of S,N-co-doped CDs, even higher quantum yields which reached values as high as 12.5%, 54.4% or 71% have been reported in the literature [21,45,46]. Even though our yield is lower compared to the latter values, it is satisfactory for sulfur-doped CDs to be used as sensitive ammonia detection agents.

To elucidate the surface chemical features of S-doped CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained. Fig. 3 A shows that both S1-CDs and S2-CDs exhibit four characteristic bands at 1009, 1036, 1126 and 1176 cm−1 which are related to the stretching vibration of OSO in SO3H group [54–58]. For S1-CDs a relatively broad band centered at 1560 cm−1 is likely related to the overlapping IR bands corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO and CO in COO- [59]. For S2-CDS a band at 1700 cm−1 is assigned to the symmetric stretching vibration of CO in carboxylic groups and/or acid anhydrates [60]. Furthermore, for S2-CDs, a broad band in the range of 1224–1517 cm−1 is likely associated with the overlapping IR bands corresponding to the vibration of OH in COOH groups and SO vibrations in sulfonic groups [61].

Even though the FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 3A were obtained subtracting the spectral contribution of water, it seems that they are still affected by its presence. The latter exhibits characteristic bands at ∼3400 and 1630 cm−1 which are related to the OH asymmetric stretching and bending modes [62]. To minimize the spectral contribution of water, the CDs solutions were air-dried and the FTIR spectra of remaining solids were obtained (Fig. 3B). The four characteristic bands assigned to SO3H groups, are once again clearly illustrated on the FTIR patterns of both dry CDs. These spectra however reveal much stronger intensity bands at 1009, 1036, and 1176 cm−1 for S1-CDs than for S2-CDs, which suggests the higher contribution of sulfonic acids in the case of the former sample. The latter groups being strongly acidic are likely responsible for the much lower pH value of S1-CDs. Low intensity bands at 602 and 1340 cm−1 for both samples are related to sulfonic groups [63]. A broad band at 3450 cm−1 is attributed to OH groups in alcohols/phenols [64]. In the case of S1-CDs, two additional bands at 773 and 831 cm−1 are assigned to tri- and mono-substituted benzenes [63]. Such configurations likely suggest a higher contribution of phenol groups in this sample than in S2-CDs. Even though both CDs exhibit carboxyl groups (1600,1560, 1411 cm−1) [59,65] a strong intensity band at 1560 cm−1 for S2-CDs and a much stronger intensity band at 1411 cm−1 for S2-CDs than for S1-CDs, indicate that the former species are predominant in the case of S2-CDs. Instead, for S1-CDs a strong intensity band at 1633 cm−1 is assigned to other oxygen surface compounds such as cyclic ethers or ketone groups (diketones) [64].

The surface chemistry of the dots was further analyzed using XPS analysis. The content of elements on the surface in atomic % and the results of the deconvolution of C1s, O1s, and S 2p core energy level spectra are summarized in Tables 1 and 2
, and in Fig. 4 . Among the two samples tested S1-CDs exhibit the highest content of oxygen and sulfur and S2-CDs show the highest content of carbon. The deconvolution of the C 1s core energy level spectra of both CDs (Fig. 4) shows three contributions at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.2 eV (two for each sample). The most intense one at 284.8 eV is assigned to sp2 graphitic carbon. For S2-CDs the smaller contribution at 288.2 eV is attributed to carbon in carboxylic groups. The contribution at 285.7 eV in the case of S1-CDs represents CO (phenolic, alcoholic, etheric). The contribution of these groups on the surface of the dots was earlier verified based on the FTIR results. Based on the deconvolution of the O 1s core energy level spectra, oxygen in both CD samples is in the form of sulfonic groups, with a binding energy of 531.6 eV. Their contribution is greater in the case of S1-CDs, which is in agreement with the FTIR analysis. For S2-CDs a contribution at 533.4 eV is likely related to OH in alcohols/phenols and COOH groups. It is worth noting that both samples show a weak contribution at 536.8 eV, related to active oxygen species. Finally, the deconvolution of the S 2p core energy level spectra clearly supports the previously seen higher contribution of sulfonic acids (at 168.4 eV) in S1-CDs than those in S2-CDs.

The EDX elemental mapping of S1-CDs and S2-CDs was carried out, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 5 . The elemental content is in agreement with the XPS analysis and S2-CDs have a higher content of oxygen and sulfur than S2-CDs. S2-Ds on the other hand show a higher carbon content. The EDX maps clearly show that most of the oxygen is attached to sulfur. This is in agreement with the results obtained by FTIR and XPS, which indicated the presence of sulfones and sulfonic acids on the surface of both S-CDs. FTIR and XPS analyzes also indicated a higher contribution of carboxylic groups on the surface of S2-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ammonia sensing on S1-CDs and S2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that exposure to ammonia concentrations as high as 300 ppm may severely damage vital organs of the human body such as the eyes, skin and respiratory tract [51], we used this specific concentration as the relevant one. The ammonia sensing/detection capability of the CD solutions was tested in terms of the dependence of the fluorescence intensity on a) the exposure time (for a constant gas concentration of 300 ppm), and b) the concentration of the target gas (for a constant time of 3 min). Figs. 6A and 7 A illustrate the time-dependent fluorescence change of the S1-CDs and S2-CDs, respectively, in the presence of ammonia. The introduction of ammonia led to a fluorescence quenching of about 1.8% and 1% for S1-CDs and S2-CDs, respectively, after only 30 s of exposure, which was therefore defined as the detection time. The maximum fluorescence quenching was found to be about 25% for S1-CDs and 16% for S2-CDs, after 30 min of exposure. For S1-CDs the fluorescence intensity exhibits an excellent linearity with response to different ammonia exposure times up to the 7th min (Fig. 6B - dashed line). Interestingly, including the results up to 30 min of exposure, the correlation coefficient decreases from 0.99 to 0.83, which is likely related to the exhaustion of the active sides of the CDs' surface (Fig. 6B - solid line). For S2-CDs on the other hand, the fluorescence intensity displayed an excellent linear correlation (R2 = 0.99) versus the exposure times even up to 30 min (Fig. 7B).

Testing the ammonia sensing/detecting capability of our CD solutions with respect to different gas concentrations (Figs. 6C and 7C), it was found that 50 ppm of ammonia led to 1.4% fluorescence quenching for S1-CDs and 0.9% for S2-CDs, with a response time of 3 min. On the other hand, the maximum fluorescence quenching was found to be about 12.6% for S1-CDs and 8.0% for S2-CDs, at 800 ppm. For the S1-CDs the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the gas concentrations up to 500 ppm of NH3 (Fig. 6D). When the fluorescence change was measured at the highest concentration (800 ppm), the correlation coefficient once again decreased from 0.97 to 0.90. For S2-CDs, the fluorescence intensity shows an excellent linear correlation (R2 = 0.98) with the gas concentration in the whole range of 50–800 ppm (Fig. 7D). Considering that both CD solutions are of the same concentrations (2.9% w/v), a higher percentage of fluorescence quenching (sensitivity of CDs) in the case of S1-CDs than of S2-CDs is likely related to differences in the surface chemical features of those samples. The results also suggest that if ammonia reactivity with the surface groups is important, the quantity of the reactive centers should be higher on the surface S1-CDs than on S2-CDs.

We have also calculated the amount of ammonia (in mg) that can pass through the CDs solutions at a) different periods of exposure to 300 ppm of NH3, and at b) different concentrations for a constant time (3 min). The results are collected in Tables S1 and S2, respectively. They show the amount of ammonia (in mg) to which 9.28 mg of the CDs were exposed (calculated based on the concentration of CDs and the volume of the solution used for testing, 3.2 mL) at our experimental conditions. Based on the solubility of ammonia (about 31% w/w, at 25 °C) [51], 992 mg of NH3 can be dissolved in 3.2 mL of water. However, the data presented in Tables S1 and S2 show that in the case of our CDs solutions the amounts of ammonia to which the solutions are exposed are much smaller than the maximum amount of NH3 that could be dissolved in that volume (992 mg in 3.2 mL). Thus, the change in photoluminescence is an indication that active sites of CDs are favorable for interactions with ammonia and these interactions result in sufficient sensitivity. The Photoluminescence measurements, presented in Fig. 6B, show the linear dependence of the fluorescence quenching on exposure time up to the 7th min of exposure to 300 ppm of ammonia (which corresponds to 0.071 mg of introduced ammonia, Table S1) or up to 500 ppm of introduced ammonia for 3 min (which corresponds to 0.051 mg of introduced ammonia, Table S2). After that a deviation from the linear response of S1-CDs was observed. Based on these observations it can be concluded that 0.071 mg could be defined as the boundary/crucial amount of introduced ammonia that can react with 9.28 mg of S1-CDs (in 3.2 mL of solution), before the exhaustion of their reactives sites starts to occur. The relationship between the fluorescence quenching by S2-CDs and the exposure time, on the other hand, does not show any deviation from a linear response even after the introduction of 0.304 mg of ammonia. As indicated above, this difference between the behavior of our two CD solutions is likely related to the differences in the quality and quantity of surface groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of S1-CDs and S2-CDs after ammonia exposure
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical reaction of ammonia with the functional groups of CDs was confirmed (or rapidly evaluated) by testing the pH value of the CDs solutions before and after ammonia exposure. As expected, for the exhausted solutions after ammonia exposure an increase in the pH was observed for both solutions. The measured pH values of the exhausted samples were found to be 9.91 and 8.70 for S1-CDs and S2-CDs, respectively (Initial values were 3.0 and 6.7 for S1-CDs and S2-CDs, respectively). Apparently, the effect of ammonia exposure on the change in the pH is much greater for S1-CDs than is for S2-CDs. This is likely related to a reaction path of ammonia with the surface functional groups.

To gain further insight into the reactivity of CDs with ammonia, FTIR spectra of the initial and exhausted CD solutions were obtained, and they are presented in Fig. 8 . The contribution of pure water exposed to ammonia was subtracted. The FTIR patterns of CDs in a solid form are not presented here since drying may release NH3/NH4 +, which play a crucial role in the detection capability of the dots. The ammonia exposure altered the surface chemistry of both CDs which is demonstrated by the marked changes in the FTIR spectra of the exhausted samples. Both exhausted CDs exhibit a band at about ∼1430 cm−1 (at 1470 cm−1 for S1-CDs and at 1430 cm−1 for S2-CDs), which is attributed to the vibration of NH in NH4 + [60,66,67]. The formation of these species is due to the acid-base reaction of ammonia with surface acidic groups such as carboxyl and sulfonic/sulfonic acids, upon their dissociation in water. S1-CDs exhibit an additional broad band at 3350 cm−1, which is assigned to the NH stretching in ammonium ions and/or amines [63]. The latter species (amines) could be formed by the reaction of ammonia with phenolic groups, cyclic ethers and ketones, whose contribution was clearly seen in the case of S1-CDs. For the exhausted S2-CDs, on the other hand, a band at 1560 cm−1 is assigned to amides [68] which could be formed by the reaction of ammonia with CO of carboxyl groups and acid anhydrates. The contribution of the carboxyl groups was found to be higher in the case of S2-CDs, based on the stronger intensity of the characteristic peaks assigned to COOH (1560 and 1411 cm−1) [63]. This was also confirmed by the XPS results.

To eliminate the effect of dissolved ammonia on the spectral features of CDs, the spectrum of water exposed to ammonia was subtracted from the spectra of the exhausted aqueous CD solutions, and this is illustrated in Fig. S2 of S.I. In the case of S1-CDs a band centered at 3620 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of OH and to the NH stretching vibrations in amines [63]. This supports the reaction of ammonia with cyclic ethers and diketones which would lead to the formation of OH and NH2 groups. The formation of the latter species is further verified by the broadening of the band at 1630 cm−1 [60]. A band at higher wavenumbers centered at 3099 cm−1 is assigned to NH3 + as a result of amine protonation by water molecules [69]. In the case of S2-CDs a band centered at 3503 cm−1 is related to the NH stretching vibration in amides, whose formation is further verified by the characteristic band at 1560 cm−1 [60,68]. The protonation of these species by water molecules is once again shown by a band at 3033 cm−1. The bands at 3250 cm−1, 1430 cm−1 and 1470 cm−1 suggest the formation of NH4 + [67], while the one at 1364 cm−1 is assigned to COO- resulting from the formation of ammonium salts [63]. The FTIR spectra of pure and exhausted water after ammonia exposure are also provided in Fig. S3 of S.I.

Based on the data obtained from the FTIR analysis, new chemical bonds were formed as a result of the reactions of ammonia with various surface functional groups of CDs. Even though the emission intensity decreased proportionally with the amount of ammonia, the emission energy did not show any change (shift of the wavelength), suggesting that the excited state wavefunction of the dots most likely was not disturbed. Several processes could be responsible for the fluorescence quenching upon ammonia introduction. These include (a) the formation of ammonium salts, as the result of the chemical reaction of ammonia with sulfonic and carboxylic acids. While more of the former are on S1-CDs than on S2-CDs, the relationship for the latter is reverse, (b) the formation of amines resulting from the reaction of phenols, cyclic ethers or diketones with ammonia (for S1-CDs), and (c) the formation of amides resulting from the reaction of ammonia with CO of carboxyl groups/acid anhydrites (for S2-CDs). Specific interactions of the ammonia molecules with S- and O-containing functional groups through hydrogen bonding might also result in the fluorescence quenching. Such groups include sulfones, sulfonic acids, phenols, alcohols and carboxyl groups. Another mechanism that might be related to the introduction of ammonia to our systems would involve the cleavage of CC bonds (Michael addition type reaction) [70]. Such a reaction would lead to the formation of amines. A schematic illustration of the ammonia interactions with surface functional groups causing the fluorescence quenching is illustrated in Fig. S4 of S.I. Finally, based on our results (Figs. 6 and 7), the fluorescence quenching could be also related to the electron accepting nature of the SO3H groups, and the electron donating character of the target gas. Such a behavior is in agreement with the results reported by Li et al. [48]. It is worth mentioning that since the fluorescence quenching caused by the chemical reaction of the surface functional groups of CDs with the ammonia molecules is irreversible, our dots are rather suitable for ammonia gas detection alarm applications (eg. ammonia leakage applications) than for sensing.

A proposed mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of the dots is depicted in Fig. 9 . In general, the excited state wavefunction is delocalized over the carbon matrix. It can be considered as having a quasi-spherical symmetry around the dot. After the ammonia exposure, the new formed functional groups can act as strong trap states where the excited electrons return to the ground state via non radiative relaxation. With an increasing ammonia concentration, the number of defects/traps caused by the reactivity of surface groups with NH3 increases and the extent of the quenching increases, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity.

Considering that in a complex environmental system, a sensor/detector should exhibit except of a high sensitivity, also a good selectivity, the latter feature was evaluated by exposing the CDs to 300 ppm of H2S which as ammonia is an electron donating gas but of a different chemistry (acidic). Various H2S exposure times resulted in a negligible fluorescence quenching effect, indicating that our S-CDs are highly selective towards ammonia (Fig. S5). This allows the usage of S-CDs in systems where distinguishing ammonia from other reducing gases (such as H2S) is desirable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and sensing capability of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore a specific role that sulfur species play in the detection capability of our CDs, we tested the ammonia sensing capability of CDs doped with nitrogen, obtained by the hydrothermal treatment of urea. The surface chemical features of the obtained N-CDs were examined using FTIR (Fig. 10 A). A broad absorption band centered at ∼3400 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of NH. Even though the contribution of water was subtracted, a peak centered at 3735 cm−1 corresponding to the asymmetric stretching modes of OH bonds, reflects its strong effect [25]. The band at 1405 cm−1 is characteristic of the stretching vibration of the CN bond [42], while the band at 1635 cm−1 is characteristic of the stretching vibration of CO in urea [71]. Finally, the bands at 1363 cm−1 and 1538 cm−1 are related to the presence of nitrogen in the form of aliphatic nitro compounds [63], likely as the result of some degree of surface oxidation. The former band (1363 cm−1) could be also related to the CO stretching vibration.

The sensing test using N-CDs was carried out. As seen in Fig. 10C and D, the introduction of various NH3 and H2S concentrations did not cause any quenching in the fluorescence intensity. Only in the latter case a very small fluorescence quenching of about 3% was recorded upon exposure of the sample to 800 ppm of H2S. The pH value of the N-CD solution before and after ammonia exposure, did not change markedly (pH of the initial sample: 9.49, exhausted sample after ammonia exposure: 9.53, exhausted after H2S exposure: 9.40). These results suggest that surface acidity and acid-base interactions between ammonia molecules and S- and O-containing surface functional groups likely govern the ammonia sensing capability of S1-CDs and S2-CDs.

Compared to other conventional electrochemical sensing and detecting systems, carbon dots as gas detectors are advantageous in not requiring electrical contacts (a drawback for ignitable environments), and in having a remote sensing capability. The detection process may not be reversible, but in certain applications this feature (reversibility) is not a necessity. In this study we are introducing carbon dots as gas detectors suitable for alarm applications (e.g. ammonia leakage applications) or applications where they can be used as dosimeter-type detectors. Since such applications are real-time monitoring applications, a longer recovery time of a reversible-type sensor could be a potential limitation of the monitoring system. Our dots can be rather characterized as environmental friendly disposable detectors, allowing the one-shot detection of various NH3 concentrations.

Similar one-shot gas detectors that show irreversible detection responses have been previously reported in the literature. Sakaki and co-workers for example, fabricated thin films of polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) doped with perylene, which they used for the one-shot detection of gaseous NO2 [72]. Upon the exposure of the films to the target gas, the detection capability of the films was evaluated by the fluorescence quenching, caused by an irreversible process. Matsuguchi and co-workers also reported a simple system for monitoring NO2 gas [73]. Piezoelectric devices coated with cross-linked amino-functional copolymers were used as the sensing devices. A chemical reaction of the target gas with the amino groups was responsible for the irreversibility of the one-shot sensing process. In another study by Smith and co-workers [74] ionic liquid–polymer 2D photonic crystals were fabricated for the visual detection of gaseous NH3. The sensors irreversibly detected gaseous ammonia and were thus found to act as dosimeters.

In summary, we have demonstrated an easy, rapid and low-cost hydrothermal approach to obtain highly fluorescent S-CDs using two different sulfur-containing polymers as the precursors. The results showed that S-CDs are very sensitive and selective to ammonia presence with a response time as fast as 30 s. We propose that the fluorescence quenching by gaseous ammonia is related to several processes, which are dependent on the chemical arrangement of the heteroatoms, and mainly sulfur. These include acid-base reactions, specific interactions such as hydrogen bonding, cleavage of CC bonds, and amine/amide formation. The formed chemical groups/defects after ammonia exposure become non-radiative trap states that partially quench the energy. Even though for both S-CDs ammonia is incorporated in the form of ammonium salts, its reactivity with the surface differs from one sample to another, which is related to differences in the surface chemistry of the dots. The most striking distinction between the surface chemical features of the two CD samples, is the higher contribution of sulfones/sulfonic acids in the case of S1-CDs and carboxylic groups in the case of S2-CDs. Both of these groups are able to react with the target gas through acid-base reactions, however the higher acidity of the former ones (sulfonic acids) increases the affinity of the dots towards the adsorption of basic ammonia. Other ways of reactive adsorption involve the formation of amines and amides on the surface of the S1-CDs and S2-CDs, respectively. Amines were formed by the chemical reaction of the target gas with phenols, cyclic ethers and ketones, while amides resulted as the reaction product of NH3 with mainly CO of carboxylic groups and acid anhydrites. A high selectivity of S-CDs to ammonia was also related to the presence of certain surface functional groups and their surface acidity. Testing the ammonia and hydrogen sulfide sensing capability of N-doped CDs, in an attempt to evaluate the importance of sulfur as a dopant, showed the lack of fluorescence quenching. This verified the crucial role of sulfur in the CDs detection capability. To the best of our knowledge this is the first time that highly luminescent carbon dots containing solely sulfur species are tested as ammonia gas sensors/detectors. The results suggest that for the ammonia detection the amount of CDs in the solution is also important and therefore not only surface chemistry but also the concentration of the sensing phase/CDs needs to be adjusted to the range of the expected target gas concentrations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the ARO grant W911NF-13-1-0225, and NSF collaborative CBET Grant no. 1133112. The authors would like to thank Prof. Stephen O'Brien, Julien Lombardi and Frederick Pearsall for accommodating our needs to use Thermolyne, 6000 furnace.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.12.035.
